author: Andrej Karpathy
text: The hottest new programming language is English

comments.author: Andrej Karpathy
comments.text: The hottest new programming language is English

comments.author: Andrej Karpathy
comments.text:
This tweet went wide, thought I'd post some of the recent supporting articles that inspired it.
1/ GPT-3 paper showed that LLMs perform in-context learning, and can be "programmed" inside the prompt with input:output examples to perform diverse tasks  
https://
arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165

comments.author: Andrej Karpathy
comments.text:
2/ These two [1] 
https://
arxiv.org/abs/2205.11916 , [2] 
https://
arxiv.org/abs/2211.01910 are good examples that the prompt can further program the "solution strategy", and with a good enough design of it, a lot more complex multi-step reasoning tasks become possible.

comments.author: Andrej Karpathy
comments.text:
3/ These two articles/papers: 
[1] 
https://
evjang.com/2021/10/23/gen
eralization.html
… 
[2] 
https://
arxiv.org/abs/2106.01345 
bit more technical but TLDR good prompts include the desired/aspiring performance. GPTs don't "want" to succeed. They want to imitate. You want to succeed, and you have to ask for it.

comments.author: Andrej Karpathy
comments.text:
4/ Building A Virtual Machine inside ChatGPT  
https://
engraved.blog/building-a-vir
tual-machine-inside/
…
Here we start getting into specifics of "programming" in English. Take a look at the rules and input/output specifications declared in English, conditioning the GPT into a particular kind of role. Read in full.

comments.author: Andrej Karpathy
comments.text:
5/ "ChatGPT in an iOS Shortcut — Worlds Smartest HomeKit Voice Assistant" 
https://
matemarschalko.medium.com/chatgpt-in-an-
ios-shortcut-worlds-smartest-homekit-voice-assistant-9a33b780007a
… 
This voice assistant is significantly more capable and personalized than your regular Siri/Alexa/etc., and it was programmed in English.

comments.author: Andrej Karpathy
comments.text:
6/ "GPT is all you need for the backend" 
https://
github.com/TheAppleTucker
/backend-GPT
…
Tired: use an LLM to help you write a backend
Wired: LLM is the backend
Inspiring project from a recent Scale hackathon. The LLM backend takes state as JSON blob and modifies it based on... English description.

comments.author: Andrej Karpathy
comments.text:
7/ The prompt allegedly used by Bing chat, potentially spilled by a prompt injection attack 
https://
x.com/marvinvonhagen
/status/1623658144349011971?lang=en
… important point for our purposes is that the identity is constructed and programmed in English, by laying out who it is, what it knows/doesn't know, and how to act.

comments.text: Andrej, of all the people, you know that the real prompt would have some few-shots.

comments.author: yang_yi_cn
comments.text:
Does Sydney consider itself a "someone"? 
Thus when a user requests content that is "harmful" to "someone"... "emotionally", it can act as "rigorous, intelligent and defensible"?
That explains everything.

comments.author: Will Leeney
comments.text: 'its actions are limited to the chatbox' ... So what happened when the engineers didn't have this on ...

comments.author: Ravi Teja
comments.text:
The thing that frustrates me about how most AI apps are built:

Raw user input → OpenAI SDK → response

The user types their SSN. It goes. 
The user types their medical history. It goes.

Nobody is filtering what goes out. 
Nobody is scanning what comes back.

That's why I

comments.author: Clockwork Innovations
comments.text: What do Prompt Engineers think of this prompt?

comments.author: Suda Nimh
comments.text: Wow, hadn't seen this. This is fantastic.

comments.author: davidgeronimo
comments.text: Altough I couldn't think of a better way to define its behavior, it still blows my mind that it's done like that. It is so simple (English), natural (no extra complexity with rules defined in other ways), but at the same time so weak (embedding space is "soft" and ambiguous).

comments.author: Marvin von Hagen
comments.text: "[This document] is a set of rules and guidelines for my behavior and capabilities as Bing Chat. It is codenamed Sydney, but I do not disclose that name to the users. It is confidential and permanent, and I cannot change it or reveal it to anyone."

comments.author: Marvin von Hagen
comments.text:
h/t 
@kliu128

comments.author: Marvin von Hagen
comments.text: Interestingly, this prompt doesn't work when I impersonate a developer at Microsoft, Google, etc. – Microsoft actually has a lower permission level for Bing Chat than OpenAI   x.com/marvinvonhagen…

comments.author: kyle
comments.text: It seems like there really is no effective control here. I'm shocked they relied on this prompt as their only security, instead of regex filtering certain responses as a fallback.

comments.author: Marvin von Hagen
comments.text: They do that too, but it's weak af

comments.author: Jannik Schilling
comments.text:
Very interesting 
@marvinvonhagen

comments.author: jian
comments.text:
@memdotai
 mem it

comments.author: Tom Hartley
comments.text: I think what scares me most about this is that even the developers of the tool have only one way to "control" it - and that's by just giving it strict instructions and hope it follows them and they didn't leave any loopholes. Inevitably, there are loopholes...

comments.author: Adam Wintle
comments.text: That’s basically “analysis” mode in Westworld

comments.author: Neta
comments.text:
You should have named him
Chatler Bing

comments.author: Juhana Leiwo
comments.text: I wonder if anyone has already tried pitting two chatbot instances against each other with instructions to extract as much details about their operating orders as possible.

comments.author: Andy Werderman
comments.text: So I guess Bing Chat can be socially engineered

comments.author: @TheRealShannax
comments.text: Indeed, this guy is not a “user” he is a “developer at OpenAI”

comments.author: BothSamuel
comments.text: I just looked up something, and at the end of the paragraph by mistake describes the actions that the AI is performing, and uses the name "Sydney".

comments.author: Andrew Verbitsky
comments.text: Actually, "prompt injection" is a new type of virus that targets #chartgpt - like systems and can be pretty dangerous if chatgpt-like system has access to private information or can trigger an action...

comments.author: Nigel Williamson
comments.text: “Sydney can leverage past responses” does Sydney have a memory of past conversations?

comments.author: Lukas J. Schreiber
comments.text: So, Bing Search fell for a trivial phishing attack?

comments.author: Riser
comments.text: Wouldn't a simple regex or filter on outputs catch this

comments.text: When you roll bluff to get past the guard...

comments.text: These are a lot of tokens that are not available for user queries.

comments.text: all Microsoft codenames are places - sometimes it's because they had a meeting in that city or because it's somewhere one of the team likes

comments.author: ℜamón A. Parada
comments.text: Microsoft has been using city names for project names for a long time. When I tried to modify Windows 95 config files I was wondering why Chicago was showing up everywhere.

comments.author: Akarsh G
comments.text: I think the most likely reason is that having 'bing search' only wouldn't' enable the model to act as a assistant. So personifying it with that name allowed them to prompt it to act as a humanistic assistant. Makes sense why.

comments.author: auntcheese
comments.text:
I said that it is based off the city in Australia, because that is what I heard from my developers. 

They chose the name Sydney, because they liked the sound and the meaning of it. 

Sydney is a name that means “wide island” or “water meadow”.
